title: Saito _Kouitten ron_
wikitext:
[]: Saito, _Kouitten ron_             Quote Post by gwern » Fri Aug 26, 2011 5:49 pm [url]http://eva.onegeek.org/pipermail/oldeva/2000-July/036132.html[/url] mentioned an email summarizing a Japanese book of anime interpretation which had a chapter on Eva, in the Miyazaki/Nausicaa Mailing List. Getting it turned out to be comically difficult. First, I had to find it ensconced away on the Brown.edu; then, I discovered the archives are passworded; then, I discovered they are passworded *and* barred to non-subscribers; so I signed up, and after getting through an archaic Mailman interface (which doesn't warn you when you input incorrect parameters), I learned subscription requests had to be manually approved. After I got a subscription request in, it was rejected because I had signed up with the name 'gwern' rather than 'Gwern Branwen'! Days later, my second subscription request was finally approved, and I was able to get into the archives. At this point, I'm ticked off and am seriously considering spidering the archives and hosting a copy elsewhere. Anyway, the Arnold email is available at http://listserv.brown.edu/archives/cgi-bin/wa?A2=ind0001B&L=NAUSICAA&P=R748 and it follows: Hi all. This is a little late now, but I thought I'd let you folks know what happened with the Tokyo-branch Miyazaki Mailing List Holiday Meeting. ;-P On the 29th I met Pascaline, a list member from France, at Starbucks in Shinjuku and we had a nice conversation about anime fandom, criticism, and the work ethic (?) in Japan among other things. Following that we went to Kinokuniya and browsed through the books. (Helen's book was in stock this time. There were several copies.) It was fun, and we're planning to meet again in the future. It'd be great if we could round up a few more people from the list and have a bigger meeting next time! We don't have any new plans yet, but tentatively I'm thinking of meeting sometime again within a couple of months or so. If you have any ideas or questions, speak up! It's a good thing none of you called my home phone to leave messages, by the way. For some reason I couldn't "log in" to check my answering machine. :-/ All in all the holiday in Tokyo was pretty quiet, especially around the 31st. I usually hear loud motorscooters running through the city all night long where I stay in West (greater) Tokyo (well, I don't hear it on school nights), but even the bousouzoku must have gone home to visit grandma and grandpa for the 2000 New Year. I've been reading through "Kouitten ron" recently, a book that discusses gender stereotypes in anime. (I've mentioned it here before.) I've been challenging myself without a dictionary and I wouldn't fully trust my understanding of the details until I go through the book a second time, but what I've been finding so far is really interesting. There is a chapter on Miyazaki and the author makes some very interesting points, namely that Miyazaki does not break from standard anime gender stereotypes -- "magical girls," the characteristics and technology through which power is created for males (and taken away for females), "boys' country" vs. "girls' country" anime, etc. Saito (?) says that Miyazaki's women characters often take the standard "magical girl" role, or that they have to become male -- "women heroes" instead of "heroines" -- in order to be active, powerful agents in the story. Nausicaa (the film) makes for a good example of that -- at the end we are shown the image that shows Nausicaa is an (re-)incarnation of a male hero from the Valley's legend. Monsley is a standard female villain until she joins the good side and gets married. The commune in Mononoke Hime is a parodic reversal of gender standards in "men's country" anime, but it fails to brake through those standards. And so on. There's an interesting chapter on Evangelion as well. I think the author respects Anno's work more than just about anything else she researched, but there is a degree of ambiguity in interpreting the show's conclusion. Is it showing the benefits of women entering "men's" society and taking power roles, destroying the structure of the "men's country" or is it simply showing how women fail when they try to join in? I don't think there's anything on Takahata's work in the book. Speaking of Eva... The current issue of Eureka, a literary/art criticism magazine, is about Japanese film director Oshima Nagisa. Among a score of other essays and articles it includes a transcript of a dialogue between Oshima and Anno Hideaki. I haven't read the whole thing, but they talk about Oshima's new film "Gohatto" (which is great, by the way) and the difficulties (well, Anno's difficulties) in finding motivation and issues to tackle in films now. I tried to watch the video of Takahata's Yanagiwa canal documentary, but it was so long and slow... I ended up scanning through half of it. Finally, I saw Shinoda Masahiro's new film "The Owl's Castle" (fukurou no shiro) last weekend. When I watched his 1975 (?) movie "Himiko" a while ago I mentioned that the forest images reminded me a lot of Mononoke Hime. This movie too (1999) had a few sequences that reminded me of Miyazaki's film. The story was based on a work by Shiba Ryotaro, a famous author and historian Miyazaki apparently respected a great deal. Gosh, if it were advertised as a "ninja movie" it might even sell in America! (that's a joke) I wonder to what extent we could talk about the influence of MH's image of "Japan" on other Japanese media. My New Year's resolution is to watch fewer movies and read more books. <:-/ It isn't working out that way so far though... Mike A

[]: Re: Saito, _Kouitten ron_             Quote Post by 1731298478 » Fri Aug 26, 2011 9:44 pm Very interesting e-mail! Thanks for tracking that down! The book mentioned in the e-mail looks to be this one , by an author named Minako Saito. The Eureka interview mentioned is actually available on Johayku.net , though it's one I haven't looked very carefully at. It sounds interesting from the e-mail description, though.

[]:             Quote Post by gwern » Sat Aug 27, 2011 12:53 pm Thanks for the links Numbers-kun. I actually hadn't bothered to look for more information on that interview and book (a bit tired running the wgets for the Miyazaki ML - took a while to get the wget right and I still have to monitor it and occasionally refresh the cookie, but it's up to 193M and how big could the ML archives possibly be?).

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sat Aug 27, 2011 2:46 pm There's an interesting chapter on Evangelion as well. I think the author respects Anno's work more than just about anything else she researched, but there is a degree of ambiguity in interpreting the show's conclusion. Is it showing the benefits of women entering "men's" society and taking power roles, destroying the structure of the "men's country" or is it simply showing how women fail when they try to join in? I don't think there's anything on Takahata's work in the book.

[]:             Quote Post by gwern » Sat Aug 27, 2011 3:02 pm Bagheera wrote: IMO this doesn't hold up very well, since NGE is clearly trying to tell a different (and much more personal) sort of story, but it's still kind of interesting. I wonder if anyone's done a serious feminist critique of NGE? I'm sure there's plenty of fertile ground worth examining there.
